title: French link sewing, the chain-link variation
subtitle: An elegant sewing pattern that links signatures without raised cords
excerpt: French link sewing produces a decorative chain of stitches visible on the spine. Each signature links to the previous through a looped stitch that gives the binding its name, the links run in a diagonal chain down the spine.
sourceNotes: Douglas Cockerell, Bookbinding and the Care of Books (1901), chapter on sewing variations: the French link sewing pattern is described as a variant that maintains structural integrity while producing a decorative open spine. The technique was in common use in French binderies of the 18th and early 19th centuries.

glossaryTerms.definition: A folded group of sheets sewn together through the fold as a unit.
A stitch that catches through the thread of the previous signature rather than passing over a support such as a cord or tape.
The anchoring stitch worked at the head and tail of each signature to tie successive signatures together.

content.text: French link sewing needs no cords, tapes, or frame. The needle links each

to the previous by catching through the exposed loop of the stitch below, producing a chain that runs diagonally across the spine. The technique suits limp bindings and soft-cover notebooks where the spine will remain visible, because the chain reads as deliberate ornament rather than exposed construction.

attrs.title: Adhesives
attrs.body: French link sewing is often combined with PVA-free, flexible-spine bindings. The stitching itself carries the text block without adhesive on the spine, a quality that makes the book open flat from the first page.

items.name: Bookbinding thread, linen (waxed)
items.notes: 4-cord or finer. French link sewing with heavy thread produces a bulky spine; 2-cord waxed thread gives a more refined result for small-format books.

content.text: Pierce and sew the first two signatures

Mark sewing stations at the head kettle, each link point (every 30-40 mm along the fold), and the tail kettle. Pierce all signatures using the piercing jig. Sew the first signature with a simple running stitch from tail kettle to head kettle. Sew the second signature the same way, working a
at both ends to link it to the first.

at each interior station. Exit the current signature at a sewing station. Instead of continuing straight into the next station, slide the needle under the stitch of the signature below, the stitch that crosses the same point on the previous signature, from right to left. Pull the thread through until a small loop remains. Pass the needle back through that loop before entering the next sewing station of the current signature. Pull gently to tighten: the loop closes around the stitch below, forming the link.

Hold the sewn text block with the spine toward you. The links should form a continuous diagonal chain, each stitch rising from the lower-right of one link to the upper-left of the next. An irregular chain usually means the needle was inserted from the wrong direction under the previous stitch. The direction of insertion (right to left) must be consistent throughout.

items.symptom: Links are loose and don't draw tight
items.cause: The thread was not pulled firmly after closing the loop.
items.fix: Work steadily, pull the thread after closing each loop, then continue to the next station. Retroactive tightening is not possible once the signature is fully sewn.

items.symptom: The chain runs in two different directions, breaking the diagonal
items.cause: The needle direction under the stitch below changed partway through.
items.fix: Always insert the needle from right to left under the lower stitch. Mark the right side of the spine with pencil at the outset as a reference.

items.symptom: Thread is cutting into the paper at the sewing stations
items.cause: Thread is too heavy for the paper weight.
items.fix: Switch to 2-cord thread for text-weight paper. Re-pierce the stations to a larger diameter if the thread consistently tears the paper holes.
